text: The video shows the uterus plastered to the anterior abdominal wall with severe adhesions, appearing grossly adenomyotic. The sigmoid colon is also adhered to the anterior abdominal wall, indicating extensive intra-abdominal adhesions.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the bowel away from the plane of adhesion, liberating the parasygmoidal fat from the anterior abdominal wall. This dissection reveals multiple fat planes, facilitating the exposure of the uterus, fundus, and anterior wall. Beneath these structures, adhesions between the bowel and bladder are visible.

The surgeon gently separates the adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall, liberating fat from bowel adhesions. The dissection progresses in a virgin plane anterior to the bladder, leading towards the cave of Retzius. The round ligament is identified as a landmark, with structures anterior to it being part of the bladder and posterior structures being part of the bowel. Fat is further separated to open the plane between bladder and bowel adhesions. The surgeon liberates posterior adhesions to the uterus caused by endometriosis, carefully avoiding surface nodules that attract adhesions.

The surgeon performs gentle adhesiolysis, lifting the peritoneum of the uterine cycle to identify and separate the sigmoid colon from the fat plane. A fine plane of adhesion is visible, and care is taken to avoid tearing it to prevent bleeding from capillaries within the fat. An endometriotic nodule adherent between the bowel and the posterior serosal aspect of the uterus is coagulated and pushed down, liberating bowel adhesions and exposing a healthy-looking ovary. The round ligament is observed to be severely distorted, forming a V shape, indicating the extent of tissue stretch and adhesion. The bowel is successfully separated from the bladder.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the bladder, starting at the uterovesical fold of peritoneum, which is plastered to the uterus and anterior abdominal wall. The peritoneum is lifted, and dissection proceeds from lateral to medial or centrally, with downward traction to tent the peritoneum and enter the space between the bladder and uterus or cervix. The goal is to separate the bladder from the uterus, focusing on a single adhesion plane.

The surgeon has opened the plane below the cervico-vaginal margins, identifying the vagina and cervix. The bladder is adherent to the uterus along a single line of adhesion, causing the uterus to be pulled up and adherent to the anterior abdominal wall. Gentle adhesiolysis is performed, separating the uterus from the anterior abdominal wall and the bladder. Electrocoagulation is used to liberate the uterus, maintaining the integrity of the uterine serosa. The bladder is completely separated, and the uterus becomes mobile, allowing for the commencement of hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy.

The surgeon identifies the ureter before coagulating the infundibulopelvic ligament, ensuring it remains visible throughout the procedure. The peritoneal and fatty tissues are lifted to maintain ureter visibility. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is dissected open, while the anterior leaf has been partially dissected, keeping the ureter lateralized. The uterine artery and vein are skeletonized for effective bipolar coagulation. The round ligament is stretched and coagulated laterally, followed by the uterine artery bundle. The bladder is sufficiently separated to allow continuation of the hysterectomy.

The surgeon separates the cervico-vaginal fascia and excises the uterosacral ligaments, identifying and opening the vaginal vault. The specimen is retrieved through morcellation, and the vault is closed with continuous polyglactin sutures. Minimal bleeding is observed in the pouch of Douglas, approximately five to ten milliliters, following the adhesiolysis.
